Metric: Software name? (string)
URL? (URL)
Affiliation (institution(s)) (string or N/A)
Response: Columbia University Robotics Group (Professor Peter Allen, lab of origin); current maintainers: Matei Ciocarlie (Columbia Robotics & Rehabilitation Lab), Jake Varley (Google), Jon Weisz; historical contributors at Columbia and Carnegie Mellon

Metric: Software purpose (string)
Response: Simulation environment for robotic grasping research; supports loading robot hand models and 3D object models to plan, evaluate, and visualize grasps; implements grasp quality metrics (epsilon and volume quality measures), contact model simulation, and the GraspIt! eigengrasp planning framework; integrates Columbia Grasp Database (CGDB)

Metric: Number of developers (all developers that have contributed at least one commit to the project) (use repo commit logs) (number)
How is the project funded? (unfunded, unclear, funded∗) where ∗ requires a string to say the source of funding
Initial release date? (date)
Last commit date? (date)
Status? (alive is defined as presence of commits in the last 18 months) (alive, dead, unclear)
License? (GNU GPL, BSD, MIT, terms of use, trial, none, unclear, other∗) ∗ given via a string
Platforms? (set of Windows, Linux, OS X, Android, other∗) ∗ given via string

Software Category? The concept category includes software that does not have an officially released version. Public software has a released version in the public domain. Private software has a released version available to authorized users only. (concept, public, private)
Development model? (open source, freeware, commercial, unclear)
Publications about the software? Refers to publications that have used or mentioned the software. (number or unknown)
Source code URL? (set of url, n/a, unclear)
Programming language(s)? (set of FORTRAN, Matlab, C, C++, Java, R, Ruby, Python, Cython, BASIC, Pascal, IDL, unclear, other∗) ∗ given via string
Is there evidence that performance was considered? Performance refers to either speed, storage, or throughput. (yes∗, no)
Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing, or any other thoughts you have)
Response: GraspIt! was the dominant open-source grasp planning simulator from ~2000-2016. Version 4.0 (April 2021) brought major modernization: switched from qmake to CMake, from custom linear algebra to Eigen3, and added Bullet physics integration. A ROS package (graspit_interface) enables integration with ROS-based manipulation systems. Activity has declined since 2022, with minimal maintenance. The Columbia Grasp Database (CGDB) integration remains a unique feature.

Metric: Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing, or any other thoughts you have)
Response: Installation is more complex than average due to older Coin3D/SoQt GUI dependencies. These OpenInventor-based libraries are not commonly installed and can be hard to find on newer Ubuntu versions. Documentation is available but installation reflects the age of the codebase.

Overall impression? (1 .. 10)
Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing, or any other thoughts you have)
Response: Correctness of grasp quality metrics is validated through the 2004 paper and academic use. Absence of automated tests and CI is a significant weakness for an older codebase. Research papers using GraspIt! serve as de facto correctness validation.

If the tutorial testing broke, was the tutorial testing instance recoverable? (yes, no, n/a)
Overall impression? (1 .. 10)
Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing, or any other thoughts you have)
Response: Core grasp planning functionality is stable for its intended use cases. GUI can exhibit instability with complex scenes. No CI means regression issues may go undetected.

Metric: Does the software handle unexpected/unanticipated input (like data of the wrong type, empty input, missing files or links) reasonably? (a reasonable response can include an appropriate error message.) (yes, no∗ )
For any plain text input files, if all new lines are replaced with new lines and carriage returns, will the software handle this gracefully? (yes, no∗, n/a)
Overall impression? (1 .. 10)
Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing, or any other thoughts you have)
Response: GUI-based interaction limits robustness testing. Some edge cases in grasp quality computation with degenerate configurations produce numerical warnings rather than clean error messages.

Response: GitHub Issues, graspit-users mailing list (Google Groups), user manual documentation at graspit-simulator.github.io

Response: User manual is comprehensive for its era. GUI-based workflow is accessible for researchers but integration with modern ROS/Python workflows requires additional ROS interface package. Reduced maintenance activity since 2022 limits community support.

Response: Declining maintenance is the most significant concern. The v4.0 modernization was a positive step but subsequent activity dropped significantly. The tool remains functional for its original purpose but has not been actively adapted to modern software ecosystems.

Metric: Is API documented? (yes, no, n/a)
Overall impression? (1 .. 10)
Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing, or any other thoughts you have)
Response: Reusable as a standalone grasp simulator for research. ROS interface (graspit_interface) enables integration. GPL-2.0 license restricts commercial use without license compliance. Dependency on Coin3D/SoQt is a modern reusability barrier.
